sanskrit_devanagari:
आपूर्यमाणमचलप्रतिष्ठं
समुद्रमापः प्रविशन्ति यद्वत् ।
तद्वत्कामा यं प्रविशन्ति सर्वे
स शान्तिमाप्नोति न कामकामी ॥ ७० ॥
sanskrit_iast:
āpūryamāṇam acala-pratiṣṭhaṁ
samudram āpaḥ praviśanti yadvat |
tadvat kāmā yaṁ praviśanti sarve
sa śāntim āpnoti na kāma-kāmī || 70 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (canonical body text; trishtubh meter — 11 syllables per pada)
exact (text body identical; punctuation differs — single danda)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.070 = MBh_06,024.070; trishtubh meter confirmed)

anvaya.meaning: desirer of desires / one who longs for desires
the one who longs (in compound kāma-kāmī)

translations.verbatim_quote: A person who is not disturbed by the incessant flow of desires — that enter like rivers into the ocean, which is ever being filled but is always still — can alone achieve peace, and not the man who strives to satisfy such desires.

Just as the ocean remains undisturbed by the incessant flow of waters from rivers merging into it, likewise the sage who is unmoved despite the flow of desirable objects all around him attains peace, and not the man who strives to satisfy desires.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Although the vast ocean is always filled with water, it is always, especially during the rainy season, being filled with much more water. But the ocean remains the same — steady; it is not agitated, nor does it cross beyond the limit of its brink. That is also true of a person fixed in Kṛṣṇa consciousness.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The ocean receives the rivers; this is its nature. It does not refuse them. But the level does not rise. That non-rise is the verse's specific point: peace is not the absence of desire-input, it is the absorption of desire-input without level-change. The kāma-kāmī (the desirer-of-desires) is, by contrast, the one whose level rises and falls with each input.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: As the ocean, apuryamanam, getting filled up from all sides; achala-pratistham, having an unmoving foundation, i.e. not transgressing its own limits; — into such an ocean, apah, waters; pravisanti, enter, from all sides without producing any change; tadvat, similarly, the man into whom kamah, desires, of all kinds, enter — assume the form of objects of vision and so on without producing any change in him; sah, that man; apnoti, attains; santim, peace, Liberation; na kama-kami, not the desirer of desires.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara is rigorous about the metaphor: the ocean does not push the rivers away (apah pravisanti — the waters enter); it accepts them; what it does not do is be moved by them (acala-pratistham — unmoving foundation). The desires assume the form of objects-of-vision and so on, but produce no change in the realised one. The person who attains peace is not the desire-suppressor; he is the one in whom desires arise without producing modification. The kāma-kāmī is the inverse — the one who actively pursues.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The ocean receives without being increased; the sage receives the impingement of all desirable objects without being increased — i.e. without any motion of pursuit arising in him. He attains peace because the objects, on entering, find no engine of pursuit to engage. The desirer of desires (kāma-kāmī) by contrast has the engine running; for him each new desirable object becomes a new motion.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja preserves Shankara's structural reading and emphasizes the engine-of-pursuit reading: peace is the state in which the desire-input arrives but finds no engine to engage. This emphasis matters — it is not that desires are absent; the desires arrive normally. What is absent is the pursuit-mechanism that, in the kāma-kāmī, automatically converts every input into motion. Vishishtadvaita locates the source of the absence in steady devotion to the Lord.

positions.rendering: ever being filled but always still
the ocean undisturbed by the incessant flow of waters
getting filled up from all sides; having an unmoving foundation, not transgressing its own limits
receives without being increased; the engine of pursuit does not engage

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All four agree the metaphor is structural: the ocean is FULL and STEADY; the waters ENTER it; the level does not rise. The disagreement is about what 'does not rise' refers to. Shankara: the ocean does not transgress its limits — boundary-preservation under input. Ramanuja: the engine of pursuit does not engage — the response-mechanism is absent. Prabhupada: the ocean is 'always still' — emphasizing static composure. Mukundananda flattens to 'undisturbed' — least specific. The structural reading that survives across all four: the ocean RECEIVES (it does not refuse the rivers); it is not moved by what it receives. This distinction is critical to avoid the misread that the verse advocates impassivity or input-refusal.

positions.rendering: the man who strives to satisfy such desires
the man who strives to satisfy desires
the desirer-of-desires; he in whom the engine of pursuit is running for each new object

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: kāma-kāmī is a Sanskrit reduplicative compound: 'desire-desirer', the one who desires desires (not just objects). The compound names a specific pathology — not the having of desire but the active longing-for-desire-as-such, the cultivation of the wanting-state itself. Prabhupada and Mukundananda flatten to 'striving to satisfy desires', losing the reduplicative force. The traditional readings (Shankara, Ramanuja) preserve it as the active engine of pursuit — the operator whose state IS the pursuit-mechanism, not merely an operator who happens to pursue this or that.

literal_meaning: As waters enter the ocean which, being filled and unmoving in foundation, remains so; in that same way the one whom all desires enter — he attains peace, not the desirer of desires.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna gives one of the chapter's most precise structural metaphors. The ocean is FULL and unmoving (acala-pratiṣṭham). Rivers ENTER it (āpaḥ praviśanti) — the ocean does not refuse the input; receiving is its nature. But the level of the ocean does not rise. Similarly, the realised one receives the impingement of all desirable objects (kāmā... praviśanti sarve) without modification of state. Shankara reads this as the desires assuming form-of-vision and so on but producing no change. Ramanuja reads it as the engine of pursuit not engaging. Prabhupada's Gaudiya reading specifies the equilibrium as Kṛṣṇa-consciousness. Critical distinction: the verse does NOT advocate refusing input or being impassive. It distinguishes RECEIVING-WITHOUT-OVERFLOW from the kāma-kāmī's pattern of rising-and-falling with each input. Peace is the absorbed state, not the avoided state.

verbatim_quote_sources: bhagavad-gita.us (Ramanuja Gita-bhasya, Adidevananda tr. — paraphrase from JS-rendered page)
remaining_gaps: Ramanuja verbatim — page renders commentary in JS; only paraphrase recovered.
